title: Convert units word problems (US customary)
description: Solve 1-step word problems involving US Customary units of measure.
topic_path: Module 2: Multi-digit whole number and decimal fraction operations
Topic D: Measurement word problems with whole number and decimal multiplication

assessment_items.question_content:
Louis made $24 \text{ quarts}$ of lemonade. He divided the lemonade equally between $6$ pitchers.  

**How  many *quarts* of  lemonade did Louis put in each pitcher?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] quarts

Convert the number of quarts to pints.

**How  many *pints* of  lemonade did Louis put in each pitcher?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] pints

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Louis divided $24\text{ quarts}$ into $6$ pitchers.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{24}\text{ quarts } \div \goldE6 ={} \maroonD{?}\text{ quarts}$

Each pitcher contained $\maroonD4 \text{ quarts}$ of lemonade.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{24}\text{ quarts } \div \goldE6 = \maroonD{4}\text{ quarts}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD4\text{ quarts}$ to pints.

$1 \text{ quart} = 2\text{ pints}$

So,

$\begin{align}
\maroonD4\text{ quarts}&=\maroonD4\text{ quarts} \times {2\text{ pints per quart}} \\\\
&= \maroonD8 \text{ pints}
\end{align}$

Louis put $\maroonD4$ quarts of lemonade in each pitcher.

Louis put $\maroonD8$ pints of lemonade in each pitcher.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bob was throwing a party. He had $22$ pints of soda before the party started. At the end of the party, there were $8$ pints of soda left. 

**How  many *pints* of  soda were drank during the party?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pints

Convert the number of pints to cups.

**How  many *cups* of  soda were drank during the party?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] cups

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Bob started with $22$ pints of soda. At the end of the party, there were $8$ pints left.


[[☃ image 1]]



$\purpleD{?} + \blueE{8}\text{ pints} = \greenD{22}\text{ pints}$

$14$ pints of soda were drank during the party.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{14}\text{ pints} + \blueE{8}\text{ pints} = \maroonD{22}\text{ pints}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{14}\text{ pints}$ to cups.

$1 \text{ pint} = 2\text{ cups}$

So,

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{14}\text{ pints}&=\purpleD{14}\text{ pints} \times {2\text{ cups per pint}} \\\\
&= \purpleD{28} \text{ cups}
\end{align}$

They drank $\purpleD{14}$ pints of soda during the party.

They drank $\purpleD{28}$ cups of soda during the party.

assessment_items.question_content:
A fish tank has $240$ quarts of water. Levi adds another $120$ quarts to the tank.

**How  many *quarts* of  water are in the fish tank?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] quarts

Convert the number of quarts to gallons.

**How  many *gallons* of  water are in the fish tank?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] gallons

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
The fish tank has $240$ quarts of water. Levi adds $120$ quarts.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{240}\text{ quarts}  + \blueE{120}\text{ quarts} = \maroonD{?}\text{ quarts}$

There is a total capacity of $360$ quarts.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{240}\text{ quarts}  + \blueE{120}\text{ quarts}= \maroonD{360}\text{ quarts}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD{360}\text{ quarts}$ to gallons.

$1 \text{ quart} =\dfrac{1}{4}\text{ gallon}$

So,

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{360}\text{ quarts}&=\maroonD{360}\text{ quarts} \times \dfrac{1\text{ gallon}}{4\text{ quarts}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{360}\cancel{\text{ quarts}}}{1} \times \dfrac{1\text{ gallon}}{4\cancel{\text{ quarts}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{360}\text{ gallons}}{4}\\\\
&={\maroonD{360}\text{ gallons}}\div{4}\\\\
&= \maroonD{90} \text{ gallons}
\end{align}$

There are $\maroonD{360}$ quarts of water in the fish tank.

There are $\maroonD{90}$ gallons of water in the fish tank.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kami's fitness trainer recommends that she drinks $12$ fluid ounces of water $8$ times a day. 

**How many *fluid ounces* does Kami drink in one day?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] oz

Convert number of fluid ounces to cups.

**How many *cups* does Kami drink in one day?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] cups

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Kami has to drink $12$ fluid ounces $8$ times a day.

[[☃ image 1]]


$\maroonD{12}\text{ oz} \times \blueE{8} = ~\purpleD{?}$

She drinks $96$ fluid ounces a day.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{12}\text{ oz} \times \blueE{8} = ~\purpleD{96}\text{ oz}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{96}\text{ ounces}$ to cups.

$1 \text{ ounce} =\dfrac{1}{8}\text{ cup}$

So,

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{96}\text{ ounces}&=\purpleD{96}\text{ ounces} \times \dfrac{1\text{ cups}}{8\text{ ounces}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{96}\cancel{\text{ ounces}}}{1} \times \dfrac{1\text{ cups}}{8\cancel{\text{ ounces}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{96}\text{ cups}}{8}\\\\
&={\purpleD{96}\text{ cups}}\div{8}\\\\
&= \purpleD{12} \text{ cups}
\end{align}$

Kami drinks $\purpleE{96}$ fluid ounces of water a day.

Kami drinks $\purpleE{12}$ cups of water a day.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jess picked $40$ pounds of peaches in her orchard. She divided the peaches evenly between $4$ large baskets.
 
**How  many *pounds* of  peaches did Jess put in each basket?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pounds

Convert the number of pounds to ounces.

**How  many *ounces* of  peaches did Jess put in each basket?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] ounces

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Jess divided $40$ pounds of peaches into $4$ large baskets.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleE{40}\text{ pounds} \div \tealE4 ={} \maroonE{?}\text{ pounds}$

Each basket contained $\maroonD{10} \text{ pounds}$ of peaches.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleE{40}\text{ pounds} \div \tealE4 = \maroonE{10}\text{ pounds}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonE{10}\text{ pounds}$ to ounces.

$1 \text{ pound} = 16\text{ ounces}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\maroonE{10}\text{ pounds}&=\maroonE{10}\text{ pounds} \times {16\text{ ounces per pound}} \\\\
&= \maroonE{160} \text{ ounces}
\end{align}$

Jess put $\maroonE{10}$ pounds of peaches in each basket.

Jess put $\maroonE{160}$ ounces of peaches in each basket.

assessment_items.question_content:
Oswaldo is cooking a big dinner. He buys $30\dfrac{1}{2}$ ounces of sweet potatoes, $13\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounces of asparagus and $20\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounces of squash.

**How  many *ounces* of  vegetables did Oswaldo buy altogether?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] ounces

Convert the number of ounces to pounds.

**How  many *pounds* of  vegetables did Oswaldo buy altogether?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pounds

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Oswaldo bought $30\dfrac{1}{2}$ ounces of sweet potatoes, $13\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounces of asparagus and $20\dfrac{1}{4}$ ounces of squash.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{30\dfrac{1}{2}}\text{ oz} + \blueE{13\dfrac{1}{4}}\text{ oz} + \maroonD{20\dfrac{1}{4}}\text{ oz} = ~\goldE{?}$

Oswaldo bought $\goldE{64}$ ounces of vegetables.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{30\dfrac{1}{2}}\text{ oz} + \blueE{13\dfrac{1}{4}}\text{ oz} + \maroonD{20\dfrac{1}{4}}\text{ oz} = ~\goldE{64}\text{ oz}$

Now, we need to convert $\goldE{64}\text{ ounces}$ to pounds.

$1 \text{ ounce} = \dfrac{1}{16}\text{ pound}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\goldE{64}\text{ ounces}&=\goldE{64}\text{ ounces} \times \dfrac{1\text{ pound}}{16\text{ ounces}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\goldE{64}\cancel{\text{ ounces}}}{1} \times \dfrac{1\text{ pound}}{16\cancel{\text{ ounces}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\goldE{64}\text{ pounds}}{16}\\\\
&=\goldE{64}\text{ pounds}\div{16}\\\\
&= \goldE{4} \text{ pounds}
\end{align}$

Oswaldo bought $ \goldE{64} \text{ ounces}$ of vegetables.

Oswaldo bought $ \goldE{4} \text{ pounds}$ of vegetables.

assessment_items.question_content:
Luke bought $256$ ounces of strawberries. He divided them evenly between $8$ different pies.

**How  many *ounces* of  strawberries did Luke put into each pie?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] ounces

Convert the number of ounces to pounds.

**How  many *pounds* of  strawberries did Luke put into each pie?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] pounds

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Luke divided $256$ ounces of strawberries into $8$ pies.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{256}\text{ ounces} \div \blueE8 = {}\maroonD{?}\text{ ounces}$

Luke places $\maroonD{32}$ ounces into each pie.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{256}\text{ ounces} \div \blueE8 = \maroonD{32}\text{ ounces}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonD{32}\text{ ounces}$ to pounds.

$1 \text{ ounce} = \dfrac{1}{16}\text{ pound}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{32}\text{ ounces}&=\maroonD{32}\text{ ounces} \times \dfrac{1\text{ pound}}{16\text{ ounces}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{32}\cancel{\text{ ounces}}}{1} \times \dfrac{1\text{ pound}}{16\cancel{\text{ ounces}}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{32}\text{ pounds}}{16}\\\\
&=\maroonD{32}\text{ pounds}\div{16}\\\\
&= \maroonD{2} \text{ pounds}
\end{align}$

Luke put $\maroonD{32}$ ounces of strawberries in each pie.

Luke put $\maroonD{2}$ pounds of strawberries in each pie.

assessment_items.question_content:
Xan and Katie are twins.  Xan weighs $6$ pounds $9$ ounces, and Katie weighs $8$ pounds $1$ ounce.  

**What is each twin's weight in *ounces*?**

Xan's weight: [[☃ numeric-input 2]] ounces

Katie's weight:  [[☃ numeric-input 3]] ounces

**What is the difference, in *ounces*, between Xan and Katie's weight?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] ounces

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
First, let's convert Xan's weight to ounces only. He weighed $6$ pounds $9$ ounces.

$1 \text{ pound} = 16\text{ ounces}$

So,

$\begin{align}\purpleD{6}\text{ pounds}&=\purpleD{6}\text{ pounds} \times {16\text{ ounces per pound}} \\\\
&= \purpleD{96} \text{ ounces}
\end{align}$

Now, we can add the $9$ ounces.

$\purpleD{96}\text{ ounces} + 9\text{ ounces} = \purpleD{105}\text{ ounces}$

Next, let's convert Katie's weight to ounces only. She weighed $8$ pounds $1$ ounce.


$\begin{align}\goldE{8}\text{ pounds}&=\goldE{8}\text{ pounds} \times {16\text{ ounces per pound}} \\\\
&= \goldE{128} \text{ ounces}
\end{align}$

Now, we can add the $1$ ounce.

$\goldE{128}\text{ ounces} + 1\text{ ounce} = \goldE{129}\text{ ounces}$

Now, we can find the difference between Katie and Xan's weight.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{129}\text{ oz} - \purpleD{105}\text{ oz} = ~\blueE{?}$

The difference is $\blueE{24}\text{ ounces}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\goldE{129}\text{ oz} - \purpleD{105}\text{ oz} = ~\blueE{24}\text{ oz}$

Xan's weight is $\purpleD{105}\text{ ounces}$.

Katie's weight is $\goldE{129}\text{ ounces}$.

The difference between Katie and Xan's weight is $\blueE{24}\text{ ounces}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sam built the same length of fence each day. At the end of $6$ days, the fence was $54$ feet long.

**How  many *feet* of fence did Sam build each day?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] feet

Convert the number of feet to inches.

**How  many *inches* of fence did Sam build each day?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] inches

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
Sam built $54$ feet of fence over $6$ days.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleE{54}\text{ feet} \div \tealE6 = \maroonE{?}\text{ feet}$

Each day she built $\maroonE{9} \text{ feet}$ of fence.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleE{54}\text{ feet} \div \tealE6 = \maroonE{9}\text{ feet}$

Now, we need to convert $\maroonE9\text{ feet}$ to inches.

$1 \text{ foot} = 12\text{ inches}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\maroonE9\text{ feet}&=\maroonE9\text{ feet} \times {12\text{ inches per foot}} \\\\
&= \maroonE{108} \text{ inches}
\end{align}$

Sam built $\maroonE{9}$ feet of fence each day.

Sam built $\maroonE{108}$ inches of fence each day.

assessment_items.question_content:
A kangaroo hopped first $3{,}520$ yards to the lake with her baby in her pouch.  She hopped the remaining $5{,}280$ yards without her baby in her pouch. 

**How many *yards* did the kangaroo hop to the lake?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] yards

Convert the number of yards to miles.

**How many *miles* did the kangaroo hop to the lake?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] miles

[[☃ explanation 2]]
hints:
The kangaroo hopped $3{,}520$ yards with her baby in her pouch. She hopped $5{,}280$ yards without her baby in her pouch.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{3{,}520}\text{ yds} + \maroonD{5{,}280}\text{ yds} = ~\purpleD{?}$

The kangaroo hopped $8{,}800$ yards to the lake.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueE{3{,}520}\text{ yds} + \maroonD{5{,}280}\text{ yds} = ~\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ yds}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ yds}$ to miles.

$1 \text { yard} = \dfrac{1}{1{,}760}\text{ mile}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ yards}&=\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ yards} \times \dfrac{1\text{ miles}}{{1{,}760}\text{ yards}}\\\\
&=\purpleD{8{,}800}\cancel{\text{ yards}} \times \dfrac{1\text{ miles}}{{1{,}760}\cancel{\text{ yards}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ miles}}{1{,}760}\\\\
&=\purpleD{8{,}800}\text{ miles}\div{1{,}760}\\\\
&=\purpleD5\text{ miles}
\end{align}$

The kangaroo hopped $\purpleD{8{,}800}$ yards.

The kangaroo hopped $\purpleD{5}$ miles.

assessment_items.question_content:
Shawntell is training for a relay race. She ran $2{,}000$ feet every day for $6$ days.

**In total, how  many *feet* did Shawntell run?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] feet

Convert the number of feet to yards.

**In total, how  many *yards* did Shawntell run?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] yards

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Shawntell ran $2{,}000$ feet for $6$ days.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{2{,}000}\text{ feet}\times\blueE6 = \purpleD{?}$

Shawntell ran $12{,}000$ feet altogether.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\maroonD{2{,}000}\text{ feet}\times\blueE6 = \purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ feet}$

Now, we need to convert $\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ feet}$ to yards.

$1 \text { foot} = \dfrac{1}{3}\text{ yard}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ feet}&=\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ feet}\times \dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{{3}\text{ feet}}\\\\
&=\purpleD{12{,}000}\cancel{\text{ feet}} \times \dfrac{1\text{ yard}}{{3}\cancel{\text{ feet}}}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ yards}}{3}\\\\
&=\purpleD{12{,}000}\text{ yards}\div{3}\\\\
&=\purpleD{4{,}000}\text{ yards}
\end{align}$

Shawntell ran $\purpleD{12{,}000}$ feet.

Shawntell ran $\purpleD{4{,}000}$ yards.

assessment_items.question_content:
Marlo buys $6$ feet of ribbon to make bracelets. She needs $18$ inches of ribbon to make one bracelet. 

**How many *inches* of ribbon does Marlo buy?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] inches

**How many bracelets can Marlo make if she uses all the ribbon she buys?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] bracelets

[[☃ explanation 1]]
hints:
Marlo has $6$ feet of ribbon. Let's turn the $6$ feet of ribbon into inches.

$1 \text { foot} =12\text{ inches}$

So, 

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{6}\text{ feet}&=\purpleD{6}\text{ feet}\times 12 \text{ inches per ft}\\\\
&=\purpleD{72}\text{ inches}
\end{align}$

Marlo has $\purpleD{72}$ inches of ribbon. Each bracelet uses $18$ inches of ribbon.


[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{72}\text{ inches} \div \maroonD{18}\text{ inches} = ~ \blueE{?}$

Marlo can make $\blueE{4}$ bracelets. 

[[☃ image 1]]

$\purpleD{72}\text{ inches} \div \maroonD{18}\text{ inches} = ~ \blueE{4}$

Marlo buys $\purpleD{72}$ inches of ribbon.

Marlo can make $\blueE{4}$ bracelets.
